children.name: Melampitta
children.description: A genus of enigmatic, ground-dwelling passerines endemic to New Guinea, formerly considered to belong to the bird-of-paradise family but now placed in their own family. They have a thrush-like build, strong legs, and a curious hopping gait, spending much of their time on the forest floor in dense montane forests. Their plumage is predominantly black or very dark.

children.name: Melampitta lugubris
children.description: A small, entirely black passerine with a stout bill and strong legs, found in montane forests of New Guinea from 1,500 to 3,700 metres. It is a shy, secretive bird that spends most of its time on the forest floor, hopping and running through dense undergrowth. Its call is a distinctive, high-pitched whistle that carries through the misty forests.

children.name: Megalampitta
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Greater Melampitta, a large, thrush-sized passerine endemic to the limestone karst forests of New Guinea. It is one of the most poorly known birds in the region, with its natural history largely undocumented. It has a powerful bill and exceptionally strong legs, adapted for navigating the sharp limestone terrain.

children.name: Megalampitta gigantea
children.description: A large, black passerine with a massive, deep bill and exceptionally strong legs, found only in the limestone karst forests of the southern escarpment of New Guinea. It is one of the least known passerines in the world, with its breeding behaviour, vocalisations, and diet still poorly documented. Its strong legs and bill are probably adapted to foraging on sharp limestone substrates.
